title: Amazon Principal Engineer On Layoffs, Interviewing & Career Growth | Steve Huynh
guest_role: Ex-Amazon Principal Engineer (~19 years), creator of the A Life Engineered YouTube channel
key_takeaways: Coding and system design tests just get you in the door; behavioral interviews determine leveling and cultural fit, so articulate your experience to match the target level.
Junior engineering roles face limited demand while Senior+ roles see extraordinary demand, and competition concentrates in pre-screening rather than the interviews themselves.
Amazon has an explicit up-and-out policy for SDE1s - promotion within a timeframe or termination - which relaxes at higher levels.
In Huynh's observation, Amazon layoffs typically hit people lacking cultural fit rather than poor performers.
The Principal Engineer promotion requires nearly two levels' worth of contribution simultaneously, with conflicting feedback along the way, so start preparing early.
Huynh's promotion project was overhauling Prime Video's outdated catalog infrastructure, a high-leverage system relied on by hundreds of dependent engineers.
To avoid layoffs, communicate directly with your manager about expectations; early transparent feedback prevents surprises.
Job-hop frequently early in your career for rapid advancement, but favor stability once you reach Senior+ where growth requires sustained tenure.
Huynh regrets not switching teams earlier and focusing too much on people-pleasing and external expectations.

notable_quotes.speaker: Steve Huynh
notable_quotes.quote: These tests just get you in the door.
